title: The Fifth Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## April 3, 2022 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifth Sunday in Lent, also called Passion Sunday, occurring six Sundays before Easter (which fell on April 10, 2022). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date typically include Ezekiel 37:1-14 (the valley of dry bones), Romans 6:8-11, and John 11:1-45 (the raising of Lazarus), focusing on resurrection and new life. The sermon falls in the heart of Lenten preparation, during a period of intensified spiritual reflection on Christ's passion and approaching resurrection.

---

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a transitional period following the 2021 General Convention in Baltimore, which had addressed several contentious issues but left ongoing tensions regarding LGBTQ+ inclusion and biblical interpretation unresolved. The Diocese of Colorado was operating under Bishop Robert Wright (who had been serving since 2013) in the lead-up to the 2024 transition period. No major diocesan crises or significant episcopal appointments were recorded as imminent in early April 2022.

---

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather with temperatures ranging from the mid-50s to low 70s Fahrenheit in the days before April 3. The city was navigating post-COVID recovery with reopened venues and events; the Denver Rockies baseball season had begun on March 31 with Opening Day at Coors Field. No major weather emergencies or significant local political upheavals dominated local news during this particular week, though the state remained attentive to ongoing water rights disputes and drought conditions.

---

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The war in Ukraine, now one month old following Russia's February 24 invasion, dominated headlines with reports of Russian withdrawal from Kyiv and mounting evidence of atrocities in Bucha and other occupied areas. The Supreme Court's Roe v. Wade decision was still three months away but was widely anticipated following the draft opinion leak in early May (though it was not yet public in early April). President Biden's approval ratings were relatively stable around 41-43 percent, and gas prices were rising sharply due to the Ukraine conflict, averaging around $4.10 per gallon nationally.

---

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Ukraine war remained the dominant international story, with NATO members convening emergency meetings and discussing Sweden and Finland's potential membership as a response to Russian aggression. China was implementing strict COVID-19 lockdowns in major cities including Shanghai, beginning lockdown measures that would intensify through April and strain global supply chains. Pope Francis was 85 years old and had recently limited his Easter activities due to knee problems.

---

CULTURAL MOMENT

America remained in a period of post-pandemic social reopening with lingering anxiety about COVID variants but widespread relief at the lifting of restrictions. There was a broad sense of geopolitical unease stemming from the Ukraine war, tempered in the US by relative distance from the conflict. The Denver area was oriented toward spring sports and outdoor recreation season; culturally, the nation was preoccupied with both war coverage and the approach of summer. Churches were navigating continued adjustments to hybrid or in-person worship after two years of pandemic disruption.
